questions.text: Which of the following is a common stereotype regarding women's work?
options: They are good at technical things
They are good nurses because they are patient and gentle
They are better scientists than men
questions.explanation: Stereotypes often limit women to roles seen as an extension of their domestic care-giving.

questions.text: Many people believe that science requires a technical mind and ________ are not capable of dealing with it.
questions.explanation: This stereotype prevents many girls from pursuing careers in STEM fields.

questions.text: Who became the first woman engine driver for Northern Railways?
questions.explanation: Laxmi Lakra, from a poor tribal family in Jharkhand, broke stereotypes to become an engine driver.

questions.text: Pandita Ramabai was given the title 'Pandita' because she could read and write ________.
questions.explanation: Sanskrit knowledge was a remarkable achievement for a woman at a time when they were not allowed to be educated.

questions.text: Where did Pandita Ramabai set up a Mission to teach women various skills like carpentry?
questions.explanation: The mission helped widows and poor women become independent through education and vocational training.

questions.text: Rashsundari Devi wrote her autobiography, the first by an Indian woman, titled:
questions.explanation: 'Amar Jiban' (My Life) was written in Bengali and describes her struggle to learn reading in secret.

questions.text: Rashsundari Devi learned to read by secretly looking at pages of the ________.
questions.explanation: She was 60 years old when she finished writing her life story.

questions.text: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain wrote a remarkable story about a utopian world where women run everything, titled:
questions.explanation: The story was written in 1905 and imagined a place called 'Ladyland' where men were in seclusion.

questions.text: Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain started a school for girls in ________ in 1910.
questions.explanation: She remained a champion for girls' education throughout her life.

questions.text: According to the 2011 census, what is the literacy rate for girls/women in India?
questions.explanation: While literacy has increased (from 8.9% in 1951), it still lags behind men (82.14%).

questions.text: The percentage of children who leave school is highest among ________ communities.
questions.explanation: Social and economic reasons, including poverty and discrimination, lead to higher drop-out rates in these groups.

questions.text: Women and girls now have the right to study and go to school. This is due to the:
questions.explanation: Individual and collective struggles have brought about changes in education, health, and legal rights.

questions.text: What is 'Campaigning' in the context of the Women's Movement?
questions.explanation: Campaigns have led to new laws being passed, such as the Domestic Violence Act of 2005.

questions.text: In 2006, a law was passed to give women some legal protection against ________.
questions.explanation: The law provides protection to women facing physical and mental violence within their homes.

questions.text: The Women's Movement campaigned against the ________ system, leading to changes in the 1980s.
questions.explanation: Protests against 'dowry deaths' and harassment forced the law to be changed to punish families who do this.

questions.text: The Supreme Court formulated guidelines in 1997 to protect women against ________ at work and in schools.
questions.explanation: These guidelines aim to ensure a safe environment for women to study and work.

questions.text: Public rallies, street plays, and meetings are part of ________ in the women's movement.
questions.explanation: It is important to spread the message of women's rights to the general public.

questions.text: When women hold up candles or join hands to support a cause, it is called ________.
questions.explanation: Solidarity is about showing support for other women and their struggles.

questions.text: International Women's Day is celebrated on:
questions.explanation: On this day, women all over the world come together to celebrate their achievements and struggle.

questions.text: Stereotypes about women can be ________ for their development.
questions.explanation: Stereotypes limit the opportunities available to women and girls.

questions.text: Why do many girls drop out of school in rural areas?
options: Lack of proper schools and teachers
questions.explanation: Distance from home, lack of transport, and absence of teachers make schooling difficult.

questions.text: Rashsundari Devi was a ________ woman from a landlord's family.
questions.explanation: Even though she was from a wealthy background, she was denied the right to education.

questions.text: What is the literacy rate of SC and ST girls compared to the national average?
questions.explanation: Social disadvantages result in lower educational attainment for girls from these communities.

questions.text: In the story 'Sultana's Dream', men are kept in 'murdana' while women have ________.
questions.explanation: The story was a powerful subversion of the existing 'purdah' system.

questions.text: The women's movement is also known as the ________ movement.
questions.explanation: It involves the struggle for women's equality in all spheres of life.

questions.text: When the women's movement takes to the streets to oppose a wrong decision, it is ________.
questions.explanation: Public protests are a way of drawing attention to injustices.

questions.text: In 1910, Rokeya Sakhawat Hossain was learning ________ while her family wanted her to stay in purdah.
questions.explanation: She was supported by her elder brother and sister in her studies.

questions.text: Literacy means the ability to ________.
questions.explanation: It is the first step towards empowerment and awareness.

questions.text: A 'Utopia' like the one in Sultana's Dream is an ________.
questions.explanation: It is a place where everything is perfect, used as a literary device to critique reality.

questions.text: According to the 1961 census, about ________ per cent of men were literate.
questions.explanation: In 1961, there was a huge gap between male (40%) and female (15%) literacy.

questions.text: The struggle for women's rights is a ________ struggle.
questions.explanation: It involves many people working together for a common goal.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT a method used by the women's movement?
questions.explanation: Active engagement is key to the movement's success.

questions.text: The law against sexual harassment was a result of ________.
questions.explanation: Pressure from activists forced the legal system to take action.

questions.text: Stereotypes about 'boys' technical minds' often discourage girls from studying ________.
questions.explanation: These biases shape the career choices and educational paths of students.

questions.text: Laxmi Lakra's father was a ________.
questions.explanation: Her background highlights that even with great odds, determination can lead to change.

questions.text: A 'Census' is held every ________ years in India.
questions.explanation: It provides vital data about the population, including literacy and sex ratios.

questions.text: The Beti Bachao Beti Padhao campaign was started in ________.
questions.explanation: It is a government initiative to improve the efficiency of welfare services for girls.

questions.text: Women's organizations also work for ________.
options: Justice and equality for all marginalized groups
questions.explanation: The movement often links with other social justice causes.

questions.text: Learning to read and write was a/an ________ experience for Rashsundari Devi.
questions.explanation: It gave her a voice and the ability to share her story with the world.

questions.text: Breaking stereotypes means ________.
options: Doing something unexpected of your gender/group
questions.explanation: Individuals like Laxmi Lakra challenge societal expectations to pave new paths.
